items.question: Define D_KL(P || Q) and state two key properties.
items.answer: D_KL(P || Q) = Σ p(x) log(p(x)/q(x)). It is non-negative (Gibbs' inequality) with equality iff P = Q, and it is generally asymmetric: D_KL(P || Q) ≠ D_KL(Q || P).

items.question: How does KL relate to cross-entropy and entropy?
items.answer: H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_KL(P || Q). Cross-entropy equals the true entropy plus the KL penalty from using Q instead of P. Minimizing cross-entropy for fixed P minimizes KL to P.

items.question: Why can D_KL(P || Q) be infinite?
items.answer: If q(x) = 0 while p(x) > 0 for some outcome, log(p(x)/q(x)) is undefined/infinite and the sum diverges. Q must assign positive probability everywhere P does for finite KL.

items.question: Explain forward vs reverse KL in approximate inference.
items.answer: Minimizing D_KL(P || Q) (forward) encourages Q to place mass only where P does — often mode-covering one peak. Minimizing D_KL(Q || P) (reverse) penalizes Q for mass where P is small — Q tends to spread over P's modes. Same P, Q can yield different fits.
